items.question_text: Under 2-2-1, when a flight plan is filed directly with the center and recorded on a voice recorder (not a strip), what must be entered in box 26 of the initial flight progress strip?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-1b, if the voice recorder is used to capture flight plan items, the controller must enter the sector or position number in box 26 of the initial flight progress strip. This identifies where the recording can be found if SAR activities become necessary.

choices.text: The pilot's name and aircraft registration.
The sector or position number where the recording may be found.
The time the flight plan was filed.
The frequency on which the pilot called.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-1, generally, how do military overseas flights file IFR flight plans with ATC?
items.explanation: Per the 2-2-1 note, military overseas flights are generally required to clear through BASOPS. Pilots normally will not file directly with an FAA facility unless BASOPS is not available. BASOPS then forwards the IFR flight notification to the appropriate center.

choices.text: Directly with the ARTCC by radio.
Through a specified military base operations office (BASOPS), which forwards the IFR notification to the center.
Through an FSS that then contacts the center.
Via telephone to the center supervisor.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-1, the purpose of recording the sector/position number in box 26 when using a voice recorder is to support _____ activities if they become necessary.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-1b, the sector/position number is entered 'to identify where the information may be found in the event search and rescue (SAR) activities become necessary.'

choices.text: search and rescue (SAR)

items.question_text: Under 2-2-2, during EAS FDP operation, when must manual forwarding of flight plan information still be performed?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-2b, during EAS FDP operation the manual actions (forwarding flight plan info and recording time of filing/delivery) are required only in cases where the data is not forwarded automatically by the computer.

choices.text: Never; all data is handled automatically.
Only when the data is not forwarded automatically by the computer.
For all VFR flight plans regardless of computer status.
Only during the hours of 0000-0600 local.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-2, what must be recorded on the form when forwarding flight plan information to ATC facilities, FSS, or BASOPS?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-2a, controllers must record the time of filing and delivery on the form when forwarding flight plan information. The note further clarifies that during EAS FDP operation, these are recorded automatically for interfaced facilities.

choices.text: The pilot's phone number and alternate airport.
The time of filing and delivery.
The controller's operating initials and sector number.
The weather conditions at the departure airport.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-2, proposed tower en route flight plans and amendments must be forwarded to the _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-2c, en route facilities must forward proposed tower en route flight plans and any related amendments to the appropriate departure terminal facility.

choices.text: appropriate departure terminal facility

items.question_text: Under 2-2-3, when must a terminal facility forward VFR flight plan data other than departure times?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-3, terminal facilities forward aircraft departure times to FSSs or military operations offices when they have requested them. Forwarding of other VFR flight plan data is done only if requested by the pilot.

choices.text: Always, for every VFR departure.
Only if requested by the pilot.
When the destination is more than 100 NM away.
Only during hours of darkness.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-3, terminal facilities forward aircraft departure times to FSSs or military operations offices when _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-3, terminal facilities forward departure times only when FSSs or military operations offices have requested them. The forwarding obligation is triggered by the request, not automatically.

choices.text: they have requested them

items.question_text: Under 2-2-4, terminal facilities must forward departure times on all DVFR departures from joint-use airports to the _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-4, terminal facilities forward departure times on all DVFR departures from joint-use airports to the military operations office.

choices.text: military operations office

items.question_text: Under 2-2-4, which type of departure triggers a mandatory forwarding requirement from joint-use airports?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-4, the mandatory forwarding applies to all DVFR departures from joint-use airports. The note clarifies that civil pilots departing DVFR from a joint-use airport must include 'DVFR to (destination)' in their initial call-up.

choices.text: All DVFR departures.
Only military DVFR departures.
All IFR departures.
Only air carrier departures.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-4, what phrase must a civil pilot departing DVFR from a joint-use airport include in their initial call-up?
items.explanation: Per note 2 of 2-2-4, civil pilots departing DVFR from a joint-use airport must include the phrase 'DVFR to (destination)' in their initial call-up to an FAA-operated tower.

choices.text: 'DVFR departure, ready for takeoff.'
'DVFR to (destination).'
'Request DVFR clearance to (destination).'
'Joint-use DVFR, (destination).'

items.question_text: Under 2-2-5, when a pilot informs the controller of a desire to change from IFR to VFR, what must the controller do?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-5, the controller must request the pilot to contact the appropriate FSS. The controller does not process the flight plan change directly but directs the pilot to the FSS.

choices.text: Process the change in the system directly.
Request the pilot to contact the appropriate FSS.
Cancel the IFR flight plan and issue a VFR squawk.
Forward the change to the destination airport only.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-6, by default, how far in advance must the 13 listed items be forwarded before the aircraft is estimated to enter the receiving facility's area?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-6a, the default requirement is at least 15 minutes before the aircraft is estimated to enter the receiving facility's area. This can be reduced by LOA/facility directive or increased up to 15 minutes under specific conditions.

choices.text: At least 30 minutes.
At least 15 minutes.
At least 10 minutes.
At least 5 minutes.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-6, when may the 15-minute forwarding requirement be reduced?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-6 opening paragraph, the time requirements of subparagraph a may be reduced when covered by a letter of agreement or facility directive. Conversely, the requirements of subparagraph b1 and 2-2-11 subparagraph a may be increased up to 15 minutes when facilitated by automated systems or mandatory radar handoffs.

choices.text: When covered by a letter of agreement or facility directive.
Only during hours of darkness.
When the controller has less than 5 aircraft on frequency.
The time requirement can never be reduced.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-6a, which item is NOT required when the aircraft is a scheduled air carrier?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-6a item 9, the ETA at destination airport is not required for military or scheduled air carrier aircraft. All other listed items apply regardless of aircraft type.

choices.text: Aircraft identification.
Assigned altitude.
ETA at destination airport.
True airspeed.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-6b, a position report over the last reporting point must be forwarded if the time differs by more than _____ from the estimate given.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-6b item 1, the position report must be forwarded if the time differs more than 3 minutes from the estimate given. This is the same 3-minute threshold used in 2-2-11 for ETA revisions.

choices.text: 3 minutes

items.question_text: Under 2-2-6a, what must be included when flight plan data is forwarded manually and the aircraft has been assigned a beacon code by the computer?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-6a item 11, when flight plan data must be forwarded manually and an aircraft has been assigned a beacon code by the computer, the code must be included as part of the flight plan.

choices.text: The beacon code as part of the flight plan.
A separate beacon code assignment message.
Only the last two digits of the code.
Nothing additional; the computer handles beacon codes automatically.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-6a item 12, what nonradar condition triggers a requirement to include longitudinal separation information?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-6a item 12, the longitudinal separation being used in nonradar operations must be included when it results in aircraft at the same altitude having less than 10 minutes separation at the facilities' boundary, unless otherwise specified in an LOA.

choices.text: When aircraft at different altitudes have less than 5 minutes separation.
When aircraft at the same altitude have less than 10 minutes separation at the facilities' boundary.
For all nonradar operations regardless of separation.
Only when the receiving facility specifically requests it.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-7, when a flight plan is manually entered into the computer and a computer-assigned beacon code has been forwarded with the flight plan data, you must _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-7, the controller must insert the beacon code in the appropriate field as part of the input message when a flight plan is manually entered and a computer-assigned beacon code was forwarded.

choices.text: insert the beacon code in the appropriate field as part of the input message

items.question_text: Under 2-2-8, what items must be forwarded when an aircraft is part of an approved ALTRV?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-8, only those items necessary to properly identify the flight, update flight data contained in the ALTRV APVL, or revise previously given information must be forwarded. The full 13-item list of 2-2-6a does not apply to ALTRV flights.

choices.text: All 13 items listed in 2-2-6a, same as any IFR flight.
Only items necessary to identify the flight, update ALTRV APVL data, or revise previously given information.
Only the aircraft identification and assigned altitude.
Nothing; ALTRV flights are exempt from flight plan forwarding.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-9, what service must be used to obtain acknowledgment that a receiving center has received a computer transfer message?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-9, when your facility is not equipped to automatically obtain acknowledgment, you must obtain acknowledgment via Service F that the receiving center has received the message.

choices.text: Service A.
Service B.
Service F.
Service G.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-9, when you transfer control information by computer message, what three items must be verified through Service F acknowledgment?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-9, the three items to verify are: (1) aircraft identification, (2) assigned altitude, and (3) departure or coordination fix time. This verification is needed unless the facility is equipped to automatically obtain acknowledgment.

choices.text: Aircraft identification, assigned altitude, and departure or coordination fix time.
Aircraft identification, route of flight, and destination airport.
Aircraft identification, true airspeed, and beacon code.
Aircraft identification, ETA at destination, and fuel remaining.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-9, what cancellation must also be verified through Service F?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-9b, any cancellation of IFR or EAS generated VFR flight plan must also be verified. This is separate from the three data verification items in subparagraph a.

choices.text: Cancellation of IFR or EAS generated VFR flight plan.
Cancellation of the computer message itself.
Cancellation of the radar handoff.
Cancellation of the ALTRV approval.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-9, when is Service F verification NOT required for computer message transfers?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-9, the Service F verification requirement applies unless your facility is equipped to automatically obtain acknowledgment of receipt of transferred data.

choices.text: When your facility is equipped to automatically obtain acknowledgment of receipt.
When the transfer is between sectors within the same facility.
When the aircraft is within 50 NM of the boundary.
Service F verification is always required; there are no exceptions.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-10a, proposed flight plans that fall within an ARTCC's PBCT parameter must be transmitted to adjacent ARTCCs via the _____ during hours of inter-center computer operation.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-10a, the Computer B network is the transmission method during hours of inter-center computer operation. NADIN is used when the route exceeds 20 elements external to the originating ARTCC's area.

choices.text: Computer B network

items.question_text: Under 2-2-10a, when must NADIN be used to forward a proposed flight plan to affected centers, even during inter-center computer operation?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-10a, NADIN must be used when the route of flight of any proposed flight plan exceeds 20 elements external to the originating ARTCC's area, in addition to normal Computer B network transmission.

choices.text: When the route exceeds 20 elements external to the originating ARTCC's area.
When the flight enters only one adjacent ARTCC.
For all proposed flight plans regardless of route length.
When the destination is outside the continental U.S.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-10b, during nonautomated operation, proposed flight plans must be sent via NADIN when the route consists of 10 or more elements external to the originating center AND the flight will enter _____ or more other center areas.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-10b item 1, both conditions must be met: 10 or more elements external to the originating center's area AND the flight will enter 3 or more other center areas.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-10b, which condition requires NADIN forwarding regardless of the number of center areas entered?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-10b item 2, NADIN forwarding is required when the route beyond the first point of exit consists of 10 or more elements primarily in fix-radial-distance or lat/long format, regardless of the number of other center areas entered. This differs from item 1 which also requires entering 3 or more centers.

choices.text: 10 or more elements primarily in fix-radial-distance or lat/long format, regardless of centers entered.
Any route that crosses more than 2 FIR boundaries.
When the route includes any oceanic segment.
When the aircraft is not equipped with RNAV.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-10b, during nonautomated operation, which of these is NOT one of the three conditions requiring NADIN forwarding?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-10b, the three conditions are: (1) 10+ elements external to originating center AND 3+ other centers; (2) 10+ elements primarily fix-radial-distance/lat-long format regardless of centers; (3) flight plan remarks too lengthy for interphone transmission. Pilot requesting NADIN is not one of the conditions.

choices.text: 10+ elements external plus 3+ other center areas.
Flight plan remarks too lengthy for interphone transmission.
Pilot requests NADIN forwarding.
10+ elements primarily in fix-radial-distance or lat/long format.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-11a, revisions to ETA information need only be forwarded when the time differs by more than _____ from the estimate given.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-11a, ETA revisions need only be forwarded when the time differs by more than 3 minutes from the estimate given. This is the same 3-minute threshold used in 2-2-6b for position reporting.

choices.text: 3 minutes

items.question_text: Under 2-2-11c, when a route or altitude in a previously issued clearance is amended within 30 minutes of an aircraft's proposed departure time, how must coordination be accomplished?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-11c, the facility that amended the clearance must coordinate with the receiving facility via verbal AND automated means. If automated means are unavailable, verbal coordination alone is sufficient.

choices.text: Verbal coordination only.
Automated means only.
Both verbal AND automated means.
No coordination is required; the computer handles it automatically.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-11c, the 'receiving' facility is defined as the ATC facility expected to _____.
items.explanation: Per the note in 2-2-11c, the term 'receiving' facility means the ATC facility that is expected to transmit the amended clearance to the intended aircraft/pilot.

choices.text: transmit the amended clearance to the intended aircraft/pilot

items.question_text: Under 2-2-11e, when a controller receives a UTM notification to an FDIO only facility, within what time limit must the flight plan data be verified to the receiving facility?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-11e, the controller must verify the flight plan data to the receiving facility within three minutes of the transfer of control point estimate. FDIO only facilities are those with FDIO but without STARS.

choices.text: Within one minute of the transfer of control point estimate.
Within three minutes of the transfer of control point estimate.
Within five minutes of the transfer of control point estimate.
Within fifteen minutes of the transfer of control point estimate.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-11b, during EAS FDP operations, when are amendment data considered acknowledged?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-11b, amendment data are considered acknowledged on receipt of a computer update message or a computer-generated flight progress strip containing the amended data. Computer acceptance of an appropriate input message fulfills the requirement for sending amended data.

choices.text: When the controller verbally confirms with the receiving facility.
On receipt of a computer update message or computer-generated strip containing the amended data.
When the pilot reads back the amended clearance.
When 30 minutes have elapsed since transmission.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-12, information received from airborne military aircraft must be forwarded to _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-12, the information received from airborne military aircraft must be forwarded to FSSs. The note explains this makes information available for relay to military bases and for use by centers in the event of radio communication failure.

choices.text: FSSs

items.question_text: Under 2-2-12, when an airborne military aircraft reports a change in destination, which of the following must NOT be forwarded?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-12b item 1, the information to forward for a destination change includes: aircraft identification and type, departure point, original destination, position and time, new destination, ETA, remarks including change in fuel exhaustion time, and revised ETA. The aircraft's current altitude is not listed.

choices.text: Aircraft identification and type.
Current altitude.
Original destination.
Remarks including change in fuel exhaustion time.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-12, what two categories of information from airborne military aircraft must be forwarded to FSSs?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-12, the two categories are: (a) IFR flight plans and changes from VFR to IFR flight plans, and (b) changes to an IFR flight plan (which include change in destination and change in fuel exhaustion time).

choices.text: Position reports and weather observations.
IFR flight plans/VFR-to-IFR changes and changes to an IFR flight plan.
Squawk code changes and frequency changes.
Takeoff times and landing times.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-13a, what is the lead time for handling proposed departure flight plans and en route estimates between domestic U.S. ARTCCs and Canadian ACCs?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-13a, the lead time is 30 minutes or as bilaterally agreed between any ACC and ARTCC. This applies to continental U.S./Canadian airspace except Alaska.

choices.text: 15 minutes.
30 minutes or as bilaterally agreed.
45 minutes.
60 minutes.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-13b, international route changes (except SIDs) must be forwarded to the appropriate Oceanic/Pre-oceanic ACC or ARTCC with _____.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-13b, the required lead time is an optimum lead time of 30 minutes or as soon as this information becomes available. Note that SIDs are excepted from this requirement.

choices.text: an optimum lead time of 30 minutes or as soon as this information becomes available

items.question_text: Under 2-2-13c, when a flight goes from U.S. airspace into Canadian airspace and returns to U.S. airspace, who is responsible for forwarding the flight plan data back to the appropriate ARTCC?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-13c, the ACC (Canadian) is responsible for forwarding the flight plan data to the appropriate ARTCC by voice transmission. There is an exception for flights that traverse mutually agreed on airways/fixes, which are determined on a case-by-case basis.

choices.text: The originating U.S. ARTCC.
The Canadian ACC.
The pilot, via CPDLC.
The overlying Oceanic control center.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-14, U.S. ARTCCs transmit flight data to Canadian ACCs using NADIN II input format for all message types EXCEPT when data exceeds the strip form field size, in which case _____ format is used for fields 1-8 of paragraph 2-2-6a.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-14a item 2, when the data to be printed on the ACC strip form exceeds the strip form field size, the Transport Canada (TC) ACC Flight Strip Format is used, inputting sequentially fields 1 through 8 of 2-2-6a.

choices.text: Transport Canada (TC) ACC Flight Strip

items.question_text: Under 2-2-14, which message type is transmitted by Canadian ACCs but NOT listed under U.S. ARTCC transmissions?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-14, the U.S. transmits: flight plan messages (active/proposed), amendment messages, cancellation messages, and response messages (acknowledgment, error, rejection). The Canadian ACCs transmit: flight plan messages (active/proposed), amendment messages, cancellation messages, and correction messages. Correction messages are only listed under Canadian transmissions.

choices.text: Amendment messages.
Cancellation messages.
Correction messages.
Flight plan messages.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-14, U.S. ARTCC response messages to Canadian input include acknowledgment messages, error messages, and _____ messages.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-14a item 1(d), the three response message types to Canadian input are: acknowledgment messages, error messages, and rejection messages.

choices.text: rejection

items.question_text: Under 2-2-15, when must 'NRP' be removed from the remarks section of a flight plan?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-15b, 'NRP' must be removed from the remarks section when the route of flight is altered due to a pilot request. This contrasts with subparagraph a where NRP is retained when the aircraft is moved due to weather, traffic, or other tactical reasons.

choices.text: When the aircraft is moved due to weather.
When the route of flight is altered due to a pilot request.
When the aircraft lands at an intermediate stop.
When the flight enters oceanic airspace.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-15, 'NRP' must be _____ in the remarks section when the aircraft is moved due to weather, traffic, or other tactical reasons.
items.explanation: Per 2-2-15a, 'NRP' must be retained in the remarks section when the aircraft is moved due to weather, traffic, or other tactical reasons. The note emphasizes that every effort should be made to return the aircraft to the original filed flight plan/altitude.

choices.text: retained

items.question_text: Under 2-2-15c, 'NRP' must not be entered in the remarks section of a flight plan unless what condition is met?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-15c, 'NRP' must not be entered unless prior coordination is accomplished with the ATCSCC or as prescribed by international NRP flight operations procedures.

choices.text: The pilot specifically requests NRP participation.
Prior coordination is accomplished with the ATCSCC or as prescribed by international NRP procedures.
The flight is operating above FL410.
The departure and destination are both within the continental U.S.

items.question_text: Under 2-2-15d, which facility is responsible for entering 'NRP' in the remarks for an international flight entering the conterminous U.S. that requests NRP participation?
items.explanation: Per 2-2-15d, the en route facility within which the international flight entering the conterminous U.S. requests to participate in the NRP must enter 'NRP' in the remarks section.

choices.text: The oceanic control center.
The en route facility within which the flight requests to participate.
The ATCSCC.
The destination terminal facility.
